coredata.dc:title: The in-situ construction of 2D electronic channel for C/TiO2 via Ti3C2 and its H2-production performance
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhongying
Pan, Meng
Zhou, Yihai
Yang, Wanping
Zhang, Xiaoxi
Yang, Renchun
coredata.dc:description: The extending of electronic transmission channel is very beneficial to accelerating the electronic transmission efficiency. Herein, the two-dimensional C/TiO2 composites with an extended electron transmission space were constructed by boric acid-induced hydrothermal method with Ti3C2 as template. The electron microscope shows that the C layer in C/TiO2 retains the original layered structure of Ti3C2. Heterogeneous interface formed between the titanium dioxide particles and the C layer, which is confirmed by HRTEM and XRD. The EPR proves that massive oxygen vacancies formed on the appearance of the heterogeneous. The results of PL spectrum, EIS and I-t transient photocurrent show that the C layer acted as electron transport path to improve the transmission efficiency of electrons. The cooperative effect of the conductive layer (C layer) and the electron trap (oxygen vacancy) accelerated the migration of photo-generated electrons, boosting an enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production performance with about 3622.7 ( ± 108.2) μmol·g−1·h−1 on the BCT sample. As a result, the designed structure showed an enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production performance with about 3622.7 ( ± 108.2) μmol·g−1·h−1 on the BCT sample compared with 0 μmol g−1 h−1 on the Ti3C2 sample. The BCT sample displayed a stable photocatalytic performance during the experimental process. It is wish that this research can supply a valuable idea for the application of the two-dimensional substances in photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D C/TiO2
Heterostructure
Charge transferring
Photoelectrochemical
H2-production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BCT and TCT composite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of Anhui Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials and Chemistry for Sustanable Conversion of Natural Resources

National Nature Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Nature Science Foundation of China ( 51572004 ), Top-talent Academic Foundation of Anhui Provincial Higher Education Institution's Discipline ( gxbjZD2021064 ), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( 2008085QB91 ), the Foundation of Anhui Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials and Chemistry for Sustanable Conversion of Natural Resources ( LCECSC-03 ) and National College Student Innovation Imbark Training Program Foundation of China ( 202210363056 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme diagram of preparing BCT photocatalyst.
(a) XRD diagram of the prepared samples, (b) Partial magnified XRD diagram of BCT.
(a,b) SEM pictures and (c,d) HRTEM pictures of Ti3C2 and BCT, (e)Mapping of BCT.
(a) XPS full spectrum of the samples (illustration is XPS spectrum of B 1s), XPS spectrum of (b, f) C 1s, (c, g) Ti 2p, (e) B 1s, (d, h) O 1s of Ti3C2, TCT and BCT.
(a) EPR spectrum of TCT and BCT, (b)The ratio of Oads/Olatt.
(a,b) UV–vis DRS and Tauc plot, (c) the Mott-Schottky and (d) bandgap diagram of the samples.
(a) PL spectra, (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra, (c) I-t transient photocurrent and (d) Production rate of H2 evolution of the samples, (e) Stability tests for BCT.
Photocatalytic mechanism of BCT for H2 evolution.

head.ce:title: The in-situ construction of 2D electronic channel for C/TiO2 via Ti3C2 and its H2-production performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, 241000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The extending of electronic transmission channel is very beneficial to accelerating the electronic transmission efficiency. Herein, the two-dimensional C/TiO2 composites with an extended electron transmission space were constructed by boric acid-induced hydrothermal method with Ti3C2 as template. The electron microscope shows that the C layer in C/TiO2 retains the original layered structure of Ti3C2. Heterogeneous interface formed between the titanium dioxide particles and the C layer, which is confirmed by HRTEM and XRD. The EPR proves that massive oxygen vacancies formed on the appearance of the heterogeneous. The results of PL spectrum, EIS and I-t transient photocurrent show that the C layer acted as electron transport path to improve the transmission efficiency of electrons. The cooperative effect of the conductive layer (C layer) and the electron trap (oxygen vacancy) accelerated the migration of photo-generated electrons, boosting an enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production performance with about 3622.7 ( ± 108.2) μmol·g−1·h−1 on the BCT sample. As a result, the designed structure showed an enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production performance with about 3622.7 ( ± 108.2) μmol·g−1·h−1 on the BCT sample compared with 0 μmol g−1 h−1 on the Ti3C2 sample. The BCT sample displayed a stable photocatalytic performance during the experimental process. It is wish that this research can supply a valuable idea for the application of the two-dimensional substances in photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the global economy entering a fast development period, the traditional fossil energy will not meet industrial demand in the future. In order to solve this problem, the development of renewable energy and clean energy is imperative. As a new energy source, hydrogen is not only environmental friendly, but also has higher energy density than the traditional fossil energy. In recent years, hydrogen produced by photocatalytic water splitting has aroused widespread concern [1–6].

In 1972, TiO2 was first used as photocatalyst by Fujishima and Honda [7]. Since then, TiO2 has become the most potential catalyst in the photocatalytic field [8–12]. However, pure TiO2 has a broad intrinsic band gap (anatase TiO2, 3.2 eV; Rutile TiO2, 3.0 eV), resulting in weak light response and low electron transmission efficiency, which is not benefit to the photocatalytic reaction. Thus, many design strategies for regulating TiO2 surface structure have been explored to improve its electron transmission efficiency. Researches showed that introducing some electron-conducting particles (such as noble metals, silicon-based material, C-based materials, etc.) [13–27] and electron-withdrawing groups (such as inorganic functional groups, organic functional groups, photosensitive groups, etc.) [28–32] on the surface of TiO2 is an effective way to improve photoelectron transmission. Among them, the C-based materials, as important substances of electron-conducting particles, have been constructed to different dimensions of 0-dimensional, one-dimensional, two-dimensional [15–24] and displayed great potential in raising the separative efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs of TiO2. Especially, the two-dimensional carbon (2D C) materials possess larger electron transmission space [21], which can greatly accelerated the electrons transmission. However, the existed researches for introducing 2D C mainly focuses on post-grafting methods. Undoubtedly, the composites prepared by post-grafting often have a loose contact structure, which is very unfavorable for the transmission of photogenerated electrons on the electron transport path of 2D C. Therefore, to improve the transmission of photo-generated electrons, it is necessary to construct close contacted heterostructure between the 2D C and TiO2.

Ti3C2 is a novel ordered 2D layered structure material synthesized by etching Ti3AlC2 with HF as etchant [33]. The research showed that Ti3C2 can be used as a precursor for in-situ preparation of some 2D-composites [34–37]. Interestingly, the resulted composites can retain the original ordered layered structure of Ti3C2. For example, Yu et al. prepared Ti3C2/TiO2 by calcining way with Ti3C2 [35]. Huang et al. synthesized Ti3C2@MIL-NH2 composites by an in-situ growth method with Ti3C2 as precursor [36]. Obviously, such special structure is beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic performance [37–39]. Recently, we also constructed a new type of Ti3C2/TiO2 composite with 2D framework by a mild hydrothermal oxidize method [40], which exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. Obviously, the composites originated from Ti3C2 not only display the two-dimensional frame but also possess the close-contacted heterostructure, which is helpful to enhance the separative efficiency of photo-generated electrons. However, among the explored composites, the electron-conducting structure all focused on Ti3C2. Undoubtedly, the two-dimensional Ti3C2 is not the excellent material for electron transporting, which is distinctly inferior to that of the 2D C. Therefore, if the Ti3C2 is replaced by 2D C via Ti3C2 as template, the photoelectron transmission efficiency should be improved greatly.

In this paper, we construct 2D C/TiO2 based on Ti3C2 template by in-situ hydrothermal method with boric acid inducing. The resulted samples still retained the original layered structure, which showed an improved electronic transmission property. Therefore, excellent photocatalytic hydrogen production performance which reached 3622.7 μmol g−1 h−1 was obtained under visible-light irradiation. This supplies a novel idea for the application of two-dimensional substances in the field of photocatalysis.

5 g of Ti3AlC2 powder was dispersed into HF solution with stirring to form a uniform dispersion. After stirred at ordinary temperature for 24 h, the dispersion was centrifuged to collect the precipitate. Then, the resulted precipitate was rinsed several times with distilled water until the supernatant was neutral. Finally, the precipitate was placed in a vacuum furnace, and the temperature and time were set at 70 °C and 12 h, respectively, to obtain dried powder. The resulted powder is Ti3C2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BCT and TCT composite
ce:section.ce:para: B–C/TiO2 composite was synthesized by in-situ hydrothermal method with boric acid inducing. Briefly, 180 mg Ti3C2, 1 mL H2O2 and 6 mmol H3BO3 were dispersed into 15 mL distilled water to form a uniform dispersion. After stirred at ordinary temperature for 30min, the dispersion was transferred into the PTFE autoclave. Then, the autoclave was placed in a air blowing thermostatic drying oven with the temperature and time set at 180 °C and 12 h, respectively. After the autoclave is naturally cooled to ordinary temperature, the mixture is transferred to a centrifuge tube. Then, the product was obtained by centrifugation and washed many times with distilled water. Finally, the composite was dried in a vacuum furnace at 70 °C for 12 h and named as BCT (B–C/TiO2). For comparison, the Ti3C2/TiO2 was also prepared and named as TCT. The only difference between BCT and TCT is that no boric acid was introduced in the preparation of TCT.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800) was used to analyze the morphology of the samples. XRD datas were obtained on X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker D8) with Cu Kα (λ = 0.1541 nm). The results of highresolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) pictures and elemental mapping were acquired on a FEI TALOS F200S instrument. Moreover, the valence states of the individual elements in the samples were revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which is equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source, and the analysis area was 0.5 × 0.2 mm2. The basic pressure of the analysis chamber is 5 × 10−9 mbar during Ar ion etching, and the angle between the Ar gun and the level is 50°. During the testing, the neutralization gun is open and the chamber pressure is 3 × 10−7 mbar. The work function measurements were performed by Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) with an HeⅠ excitation energy as 21.22 eV, and toroidal grating monochromators (TGM) monochromator was used during the experiment. According to L. Hultman's studies [41–45], the binding energy scale was corrected by measuring all core-level spectra and work function ΦSA (Table S1) of the samples using XPS and UPS measurements, respectively. The work function ΦSA of Ti3C2, TCT and BCT is 3.55 eV, 4.98 eV, 5.08 eV, respectively. Then, C1s peak position was corrected using the formula EB = 289.58–ΦSA. The peak position of C 1s of Ti3C2, TCT and BCT is calibrated to 286.07 eV, 284.6 eV, 284.5 eV, respectively. The calculated the required shift by taking the difference between measured C 1s binding energy and EB (Table S1), and then applied the shift to all the core levels. UV–vis diffffuse reflflectance spectroscopy (DRS) on Shimadzu UV 3600 was utilized to judge the photoabsorption capability of the samples. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for samples were analyzed on a Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7100 FL. Photoelectrochemical tests were conducted on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). The system utilizing a three–electrode system with the photocatalyst covered ITO glass, saturated Ag/AgCl and platinum electrode as the working electrode, the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. Visible light comes from a 300W Xenon lamp (AM-1.5G). The preparation procedure of the working electrode is as follows: Fristly, 4 mg of catalyst was scattered into 0.8 mL of distilled water. After supersonic treatment for 30min, 40 μL of Nafion was added into the resulted slurry, and then supersonic treatment was continued for 30min. Finally, the slurry was equably coated on an ITO glass substrate (1 × 2 cm2). The photoelectrochemical property of the catalysts were tested in 0.5 mol L−1 Na2SO4 electrolyte.

The photocatalytic hydrogen production performance was evaluated by Labsolar-21 AG system. The visible light was obtained by using a 300W Xenon lamp (AM-1.5G). Briefly, 50 mg of photocatalyst was added into 125 mL aqueous solution, which contained 25 mL of CH3OH as sacrificial reagent by ultrasonic treatment. 1 wt% platinum was supported on photocatalyst as co-catalyst by photo-deposition. During the reaction, the system was in a vacuum state, and the reaction temperature was remained at 15 °C. The magnetic stirrer is used for continuous stirring to keep the catalyst in uniform dispersion. The production of H2 was estimated on a gas chromatograph (GC7900) with a TCD sensor every 2 h, and purge with Ar gas.

The preparation procedure of the BCT photocatalyst is diagrammed in Fig. 1 . Firstly, Al atoms in Ti3AlC2 were etched away after hydrofluoric acid etching and then the Ti3C2 formed. After that, the Ti3C2 was in-situ oxidized with H3BO3 and H2O2. Finally, the two-dimensional B-doped C/titanium dioxide (B–C/TiO2) was obtained, which was named as BCT. Boric acid can provide a mild acidic environment for hydrothermal system, which intensifies the efficiency of in-situ oxidation reaction. As a result, titanium ions were precipitated to form titanium dioxide with boric acid inducing, while the 2D C acted as a skeleton to support the TiO2 particles. Eventually, Ti3C2 was transformed to the B–C/TiO2 (BCT).

Fig. 2 a is the XRD diagram of the prepared samples. Obviously, compared with Ti3AlC2, the no diffraction peak at 39° can be found on Ti3C2 sample. Since the diffraction peak at 39° is the most significant characteristic line for Ti3AlC2, demonstrating that the Ti3AlC2 has been transformed to Ti3C2 MXene. After hydrothermal and boric acid-induced hydrothermal, the TCT and BCT samples all exhibited the anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272) with three characteristic diffraction peaks at 25.31°, 37.87° and 47.99°. Perceptibly, the intensity of TiO2 diffraction peaks on BCT are stronger than those of TCT, revealing that titanium ions are easier to being precipitated to form TiO2 when boric acid was introduced in hydrothermal process. Based on JADE calculation, the sizes of the BCT particles with different crystal faces (100), (200) and (004) are 27.3, 29.6, 38.6 nm, which are all nearly smaller than those crystal faces of TCT particles 33.2, 30.4, 37.4 nm, indicating that the BCT obtained some smaller particles, which may be easily to promote the photo catalysis performance [46–48]. Importantly, the characteristic diffraction peaks of C (JCPDS no.50-0926) can be found on the partial magnified XRD patterns of the BCT sample (Fig. 2b), suggesting that Ti3C2 was transformed into C/TiO2 successfully with boric acid inducing.

Fig. 3 a is the SEM pictures of Ti3C2. It shows that Ti3C2 has multi-layer structure and slick surface, indicating that Al layer has been etched away successfully. The HRTEM pictures (Fig. 3c) of Ti3C2 showed the (002) crystal plane lattice spacing with 1.17 nm, indicating that the 2D layer structure formed. As shown in Fig. 3b, after boric acid induced hydrothermal, it can be seen that the C layer of BCT still retains the layered structure of Ti3C2 in the red box region. From Fig. 3d, the HRTEM pictures shows the particle with two different lattice fringes of 0.35 and 0.19 nm, which are corresponding to (101) and (200) crystal plane of the TiO2, respectively. In addition, the C layer can be clearly observed, which is also confirmed by the foregoing XRD result. Obviously, a close heterojunction is formed between C and TiO2 in BCT. However, for the TCT sample (Fig. S1), only TiO2 particles (d-space = 0.35 nm) and original template Ti3C2 (d-space = 0.23 nm) can be observed. No C layer can be found. The above results confirmed that the boric acid acted as an important inducer to the formation of C layer. The excellent conductivity of C layer may be conducive to accelerate the electron transmission and improving the photocatalytic performance. From EDS mapping of Fig. 3e, the BCT composite contains B, Ti, C and O elements, which demonstrates that boron is also doped into the sample during boric acid inducing hydrothermal process.

The XPS binding energy of Ti3C2, TCT and BCT are shown in Fig. 4 . From the full spectrums in Fig. 4a, the three samples all showed C, Ti and O peaks, whereas a new peak of B 1s appeared on the BCT sample. Before analyzing high-resolution spectra of individual elements are analyzed, they are calibrated to weaken the bias caused by adventitious carbon and test conditions [41–45]. Herein, the corrected binding energy of the C–C component of C 1s for Ti3C2 is 286.07 eV (for a specific calibration process, reference to Fig. S2, Table S1 and corresponding descriptions), and other elements are subjected to the same normalization accordingly. For the template Ti3C2 sample (Fig. 4b), the C 1s spectrum of Ti3C2 with two components at 286.07 eV (C–C), and 284.46 eV (C–Ti-Ox) are attributed to Ti3C2 and indefinite carbon, respectively [49]. As displayed in Fig. 4c, six different peaks can be found at 456.07, 457.95, 460.21, 461.83, 464.19 and 465.92 eV in the Ti 2p spectrum of Ti3C2, which are matched to the Ti–C 2p3/2, Ti–O 2p3/2, Ti4+ 2p3/2, Ti–C 2p1/2, Ti–O 2p1/2, Ti4+ 2p1/2, respectively [50]. The O 1s spectrum of Ti3C2 was shown in Fig. 4d. The peaks at 530.19, 531.44, and 533.1 eV, can be deconvoluted into Ti–O, C–Ti-Ox, and C–Ti-OHx, respectively [51]. After boric acid inducing hydrothermal, a peak of B 1s appeared on the BCT sample. According to the reported literatures, the boric acid inducing process is often ascribed to weak oxidation effect of boric acid [52]. As displayed in Fig. 4e, a peak can be found at 191.88 eV in the B 1s spectrum of BCT, which are matched to the B–O–Ti, and also B–O–B [53]. In Fig. 4f, the TCT and BCT samples exhibit peak of C–C at 284.6 eV and 284.5 eV, respectively, which is due to the C 1s signal closely follows the variation of sample work function фSA [42]. For the TCT sample, the C–Ti-Ox bond at 283.00 eV can be observed, indicating that the part of Ti3C2 was still retained during the hydrothermal process. However, for the BCT sample, the C–Ti-Ox bond (283.00 eV) disappeared while a new peak at 285.30 eV appeared. Such peak is attributed to C–O bonds. Compared the results of BCT with TCT, it is not difficult to find that the C–Ti-Ox bond of Ti3C2 MXene was transformed to C–O by hydrothermal treatment with boric acid. Therefore, it is very beneficial to the formation of C and the precipitation of titanium ions when the boric acid was introduced. Obviously, the intensity of Ti peak of BCT is stronger than that of TCT in Fig. 4g, indicating that the BCT sample is rich in titanium dioxide. Such result proved that it is propitious to the generation of TiO2 from the precipitated titanium ions with boric acid inducing. Compared with TCT, BCT exhibited 0.2 eV positive migration on Ti 2p energy band orbitals, which can be attributed to the change of their chemical environment by the tight contact between C and TiO2. In Fig. 4h, the O 1s of BCT and TCT display a similar result as that of the Ti 2p. The peak intensities of lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen in BCT are higher than those in TCT, suggesting that more titanium dioxide formed by the promotion of boric acid. This also be proved by the quantified contents of oxygen atoms for TCT (23.23%) and BCT (40.34%) by XPS in Table S2. Equally, BCT showed 0.2 eV positive migration on O 1s energy band orbitals compared with TCT, which is also attribute to the tight contact between C and TiO2.

EPR analysis was used to further qualitatively confirm the oxygen vacancy. As shown in Fig. 5 a, TCT and BCT have a characteristic peak centered at g = 2.003, indicating the oxygen vacancies were formed [41]. From the difference of peak intensities, it can be inferred that the oxygen vacancy content in BCT is higher than that in TCT. Furthermore, the ratio of lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen of TCT and BCT were calculated by peak splitting data of O 1s XPS. The results are shown in Fig. 5b，the ratios of Oads/Olatt are 0.35 and 0.49, respectively, which is consistent with the above EPR results. Such result comfirm that boric acid inducing is convenient for the formation of surface oxygen vacancy.

To study the photoabsorption characteristics, the UV–vis DRS (Fig. 6 a) of the Ti3C2,TCT and BCT are monitored at 200–700 nm. In the range of 200–700, the photoabsorption of Ti3C2 sample is obvious because of its black colour nature [38]. After hydrothermal treatment, the photoabsorption range of TCT sample is narrower than that of Ti3C2, substantiating that a part of Ti3C2 was oxidized to form TiO2. Moreover, the absorption edge of BCT show a slightly lower position than that of TCT, which is closed to that of pure titanium dioxide [11]. Such result proves that more titanium dioxide was formed by boric acid inducing. The band gap energy (Eg) was calculated by the formula of (ahv)2 = K(hv-Eg), the results are showed in Fig. 6b. The Eg values of Ti3C2,TCT and BCT are around 2.10 eV, 2.51 eV and 2.86 eV, respectively. Obviously, according to the Eg values, the Ti3C2 is still as a non-semiconductor, whereas TCT and BCT have been converted to semiconductor composites.

Mott-Schottky (M − S) image is displayed in Fig. 6c. It is observed that the flat band potential (Efb) of Ti3C2,TCT and BCT were recorded as −0.6, −0.45, and −0.42 eV versus the Ag/AgCl electrode, respectively. After conversion, the Efb values of the samples are −0.4, −0.25, and −0.22 eV versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). It is well known that the slope of M − S curve of n-type semiconductor is positive. According to the slope of curves in Fig. 6c, it can be judged that all samples are n-type semiconductors. The conduction band energy (ECB) of n-type semiconductors is higher than Efb about with −0.1–0.2 eV [54]. Therefore, the ECB values of Ti3C2, TCT and BCT are calculated to be −0.6, −0.45 and −0.42 eV (versus NHE). Combined with the Eg of samples (Fig. 6b), the VB of Ti3C2,TCT and BCT are estimated to be 1.50, 2.06, and 2.44 eV. The bandgap diagram of the samples is shown in Fig. 6d. The CB position of samples are all lower than the hydrogen production potential (0eV versus NHE), proving that resulted samples have the capacity to reduce H2O to H2 [55,56]. Notably, the CB location of BCT is more positive than that of TCT, indicating that BCT is more favorable for hydrogen production than TCT.

The PL spectra of Ti3C2,TCT and BCT are displayed in Fig. 7 a. Remarkably, compared with Ti3C2 and TCT, the emission peak intensity of BCT is reduced, proving that the separative efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in BCT is higher than that of Ti3C2 and TCT. In Fig. 7b, the size of arc for TCT and BCT are larger than that of Ti3C2, implying that the impedance of TCT and BCT is higher than that of Ti3C2. Such result proves that Ti3C2 is a non-semiconductor. In addition, compared with TCT, BCT has a lower impedance, which is imputed to the generation of the 2D C layer with ultra-high conduction in the BCT sample.

The i-t transient photocurrent of Ti3C2,TCT and BCT are displayed in Fig. 7c. Obviously, the photocurrent response of Ti3C2 is extremely low, indicating that the Ti3C2 is not be apt to promote the separation of photo-generated carrier [57]. Nevertheless, the conspicuous photocurrent emerged on TCT and BCT, demonstrating that the generation of Ti3C2/TiO2 and C/TiO2 heterostructures is propitious to the rapid migration of excited electrons. More importantly, the photocurrent response ability of BCT is obviously stronger than that of TCT, indicating that compared with Ti3C2 in Ti3C2/TiO2, the 2D C layer in C/TiO2 is more beneficial to promote the separation of photo-generated carrier.

The H2 production capacities of Ti3C2,TCT and BCT samples are showed in Fig. 7d. Evidently, the generation rates of H2 are around 0, 2520.4, and 3622.7 μmol g−1 h−1 for Ti3C2,TCT and BCT, respectively. The photocatalytic H2 production capacity of Ti3C2 is almost absent due to that Ti3C2 is a non-semiconductor material. After hydrothermal reaction, titanium dioxide was formed in TCT and BCT samples, resulting in an distinct photocatalytic performance. Obviously, the photocatalytic H2 production performance of BCT is stronger than that of TCT, which can be ascribed to the reality that the conductivity of C layer in BCT is higher than that of the Ti3C2 in TCT. The excellent conductivity of C layer is conducive to accelerat the electron transmission and improving the photocatalytic performance. In addition, the photocatalytic system was cycled six times within 24 h for study the stability of BCT samples (Fig. 7e). Perceptibly, the H2 production rate of BCT sample does not decrease obviously, indicating that the catalyst is relatively stable.

Through the analysis and discussion of the above experimental results, the feasible photocatalytic mechanism of the catalyst BCT in photocatalytic hydrogen production system is raised in Fig. 8 . Firstly, the TiO2 produced by in-situ oxidation is used as a semiconductor material. The C layer originated from Ti3C2 not only acted as conductive layer to accelerate electron transmission, but also regulated the energy band structure of C/TiO2 to form a favorable potential for hydrogen production. Therefore, the C/TiO2 displayed suitable positions of VB and CB for hydrogen production. Under the irradiation of visible light, the electrons in VB are stimulated more easily and then transmitted to CB. The holes left in VB are expended by sacrificial agents, while the electrons rapidly migrated by the C layer to CB and then combined with H+ in water to generate hydrogen. Another important structure, oxygen vacancy, also plays an important role in electron transport. Some titanium ions were not completely oxidized with boric acid inducing, resulting in the formation of oxygen vacancies. As an electron trap, oxygen vacancy further strengthened the separation and the transmission of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [58], which is contributed to the improvement of photocatalytic hydrogen production performance.

To sum up, the 2D C layer/TiO2 photocatalyst constructed by boric acid-induced hydrothermal method provides a novel idea for the application of 2D substances in photocatalysis. The intimate contact between C layer and TiO2 is beneficial to the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The 2D C layer and oxygen vacancy of the original Ti3C2 structure are retained, which provides great convenience for the light-induced electron transfer in the photocatalytic system. The prepared samples have suitable potential for hydrogen production and low recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. Under the same test conditions, BCT has the best hydrogen production performance among the three samples in this experiment. The hydrogen evolution of BCT is 3622.7 ( ± 108.2) μmol·g-1·h-1 under simulated sunlight irradiation for 2 h. In addition, BCT samples also showed good stability in photocatalytic cycling experiments. Based on the above experimental results and theoretical analysis, a feasible mechanism is raised to explain the enhanced hydrogen evolution activity of the BCT sample.

Zhongying Liu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Meng Pan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yihai Zhou: Validation. Wanping Yang: Resources. Xiaoxi Zhang: Validation, Funding acquisition. Renchun Yang: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Nature Science Foundation of China (51572004), Top-talent Academic Foundation of Anhui Provincial Higher Education Institution's Discipline (gxbjZD2021064), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2008085QB91), the Foundation of Anhui Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials and Chemistry for Sustanable Conversion of Natural Resources (LCECSC-03) and National College Student Innovation Imbark Training Program Foundation of China (202210363056).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vacuum.2023.112229.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly ordered nitrogen-rich mesoporous carbon nitrides and their superior performance for sensing and photocatalytic hydrogen generation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.P. Mane, S.N. Talapaneni, K.S. Lakhi, H. Ilbeygi, U. Ravon, K. Al-Bahily, T. Mori, D.-H. Park, A. Vinu, Highly ordered nitrogen-rich mesoporous carbon nitrides and their superior performance for sensing and photocatalytic hydrogen generation, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 56 (2017) 8481-8485.

Tsang, Photocatalytic water splitting by N-TiO2 on MgO (111) with exceptional quantum effificiencies at elevated temperatures, Nat. Commun. 10 (2019) 4421.
